source_lists.title: New Hollywood
source_lists.desc:
From Wikipedia:

New Hollywood or post-classical Hollywood, sometimes referred to as the "American New Wave", refers to the time from roughly the late-1960s (Bonnie and Clyde, The Graduate) to the early 1980s (Heaven's Gate, One from the Heart) when a new generation of young filmmakers came to prominence in America, influencing the types of films produced, their production and marketing, and the way major studios approached filmmaking. In New Hollywood films, the film director took on a key authorial role.

The films they made were part of the studio system, and although these individuals were not "independent filmmakers", they introduced subject matter and styles that set them apart from the studio traditions that an earlier generation had established ca. 1920s–1950s. New Hollywood has also been defined as a broader filmmaking movement influenced by this period, which has been called the "Hollywood renaissance".

The following is a chronological list of those films that are generally considered to be "New Hollywood" productions.

source_lists.title: New Hollywood
source_lists.desc: New Hollywood was a contingent of American filmmakers, influenced by foreign art cinema (e.g. French New Wave), who rose to prominence in the late 1960s both within and outside of the Hollywood studio system. The movement was brought upon as a reaction to the declining interest in epics, musicals, and other standard Hollywood fanfare of the time.

source_lists.title: New Hollywood
source_lists.desc: Generally regarded to have broken out with 1967's Bonnie and Clyde, New Hollywood—also known as the New American Cinema or the Hollywood Renaissance—saw the rise of several prominent filmmakers who emerged in the wake of the studio system's gradual crumbling. It was a time of great cinematic daring for the American cinema, a multitude of difficult themes suddenly rushing to the fore of mainstream movies along with heretofore unseen levels of violence and sexuality. Weakened by the birth of the blockbuster in Jaws and Star Wars, it is considered to have died out in the aftermath of Heaven's Gate, when the increasingly massive budgets these American auteurs had been afforded produced movies of increasing indulgence and dwindling returns. My earliest… more

source_lists.title: new hollywood
source_lists.desc:
bc i am the lamest person of all time this is inspired by crit channel's recent new hollywood selection. if there is anything online film dorks need, it is more discussion of 70s movies (.../s)!!

must be
1) american produced/directed
2) made between 1967-1982
3) fit a particular vibe - love willy wonka and the chocolate factory/star wars/grease/etc. but that's not really new hollywood to me if you catch my drift
4) not including shorts or docs

source_lists.title: New Hollywood
source_lists.desc:
An American New Wave
----------------------------------
I originally made this list in 2018 when the New Hollywood era was my absolute favourite period in film history. I now think I was too liberal with my definition of what films constituted 'New Hollywood'. Instead of cutting any films from this list though I have decided to go back to add a few films/filmmakers that I have since discovered and should be a part of this list. (April, 2024)
